quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Probability density function (PDF)"?

options.text: ∫u dv = uv - ∫v du; derived from the product rule: d(uv) = u dv + v du
To find absolute extrema on [a,b]: 1) find critical points, 2) evaluate f at critical points and endpoints, 3) compare values
Priority for choosing u: Logarithmic > Inverse trig > Algebraic > Trigonometric > Exponential; u is differentiated, dv is integrated
f(x) ≥ 0 with ∫₋∞^∞ f(x)dx = 1; the area under f over [a,b] gives P(a ≤ X ≤ b)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Probability density function (PDF)" is defined as: f(x) ≥ 0 with ∫₋∞^∞ f(x)dx = 1; the area under f over [a,b] gives P(a ≤ X ≤ b). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Cumulative distribution function (CDF)"?

options.text: f''(x) > 0; the graph curves upward like a cup; the slope is increasing
Motion along a straight line (one dimension); described completely by position as a function of time s(t)
d/dx[f(x)/g(x)] = [f'(x)g(x) - f(x)g'(x)] / [g(x)]²; 'low d-high minus high d-low over low squared'
F(x) = P(X ≤ x) = ∫₋∞ˣ f(t)dt; F'(x) = f(x) by FTC; F ranges from 0 to 1

quiz_questions.explanation: "Cumulative distribution function (CDF)" is defined as: F(x) = P(X ≤ x) = ∫₋∞ˣ f(t)dt; F'(x) = f(x) by FTC; F ranges from 0 to 1. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Expected value (mean)"?

options.text: μ = E[X] = ∫₋∞^∞ x·f(x)dx; the 'center of mass' of the probability distribution
The set of input values that make physical sense in the context; endpoints may produce absolute extrema
[f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a); the average rate of change over [a,b]; the slope of the line connecting endpoints
d/dx[cf(x)] = c·f'(x); constants factor out of the derivative

quiz_questions.explanation: "Expected value (mean)" is defined as: μ = E[X] = ∫₋∞^∞ x·f(x)dx; the 'center of mass' of the probability distribution. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Variance"?

options.text: σ² = E[(X-μ)²] = ∫₋∞^∞ (x-μ)²f(x)dx; measures spread around the mean
Left: sample at left endpoint; Right: sample at right endpoint; Midpoint: sample at center of each subinterval
MVT + IVT: first use MVT to show f' achieves certain values, then use IVT on f' to show intermediate values exist
To find absolute extrema on [a,b]: 1) find critical points, 2) evaluate f at critical points and endpoints, 3) compare values

quiz_questions.explanation: "Variance" is defined as: σ² = E[(X-μ)²] = ∫₋∞^∞ (x-μ)²f(x)dx; measures spread around the mean. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Normal distribution"?

options.text: Left-hand limit: lim(x→c⁻) f(x); right-hand limit: lim(x→c⁺) f(x); the two-sided limit exists only if both one-sided limits are equal
Local min: concave up valley (f'' > 0); local max: concave down hill (f'' < 0)
f(x) = (1/(σ√(2π)))e^(-(x-μ)²/(2σ²)); the bell curve; its integral (CDF) has no closed form
Priority for choosing u: Logarithmic > Inverse trig > Algebraic > Trigonometric > Exponential; u is differentiated, dv is integrated

quiz_questions.explanation: "Normal distribution" is defined as: f(x) = (1/(σ√(2π)))e^(-(x-μ)²/(2σ²)); the bell curve; its integral (CDF) has no closed form. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "f(x) ≥ 0 with ∫₋∞^∞ f(x)dx = 1; the area under f over [a,b] gives P(a ≤ X ≤ b)"?

options.text: Probability density function (PDF)
Concavity of F
When the test fails
Left/Right/Midpoint sum

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Probability density function (PDF)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "F(x) = P(X ≤ x) = ∫₋∞ˣ f(t)dt; F'(x) = f(x) by FTC; F ranges from 0 to 1"?

options.text: Tangent lines to implicit curves
Integration by parts formula
Cumulative distribution function (CDF)
When the test fails

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Cumulative distribution function (CDF)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "μ = E[X] = ∫₋∞^∞ x·f(x)dx; the 'center of mass' of the probability distribution"?

options.text: Expected value (mean)
Simplify before differentiating
Equilibrium solutions
d/dx[f(x)^g(x)]

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Expected value (mean)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "σ² = E[(X-μ)²] = ∫₋∞^∞ (x-μ)²f(x)dx; measures spread around the mean"?

options.text: Variance
Simplify before differentiating
One-sided limits
Inflection point at P = K/2

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Variance".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "f(x) = (1/(σ√(2π)))e^(-(x-μ)²/(2σ²)); the bell curve; its integral (CDF) has no closed form"?

options.text: Second derivative confirmation
Domain restrictions
Table-based accumulation
Normal distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Normal distribution".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Probability Distributions is accurate?

options.text: Derivatives represent rates of change in any context: population growth rates, velocity, temperature change rates, marginal economic quantities, and reaction rates.
The product rule extends to three or more factors: d/dx[fgh] = f'gh + fg'h + fgh'.
Probability Distributions connects integration to probability theory.
Arc length of y = f(x) from a to b: L = ∫ₐᵇ √(1 + [f'(x)]²)dx.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Probability Distributions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Probability Distributions?

options.text: A continuous probability density function (PDF) f(x) satisfies f(x) ≥ 0 and ∫₋∞^∞ f(x)dx = 1.
When k > 0: exponential growth (population, investment); when k < 0: exponential decay (radioactive decay, drug clearance).
A Riemann sum divides [a,b] into n subintervals and approximates the area using rectangles: Σᵢf(xᵢ*)Δx.
Linear approximation uses the tangent line to estimate function values near a known point: f(x) ≈ f(a) + f'(a)(x-a).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Probability Distributions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Probability Distributions?

options.text: Solutions are functions y(x) that satisfy the equation.
The probability that a random variable X falls in [a,b] is P(a ≤ X ≤ b) = ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx.
The product rule extends to three or more factors: d/dx[fgh] = f'gh + fg'h + fgh'.

Key properties: ∫ₐᵇ [f ± g]dx = ∫ₐᵇ f dx ± ∫ₐᵇ g dx (linearity); ∫ₐᵇ cf dx = c∫ₐᵇ f dx (constant multiple); ∫ₐᵇ f dx + ∫ᵇᶜ f dx = ∫ₐᶜ f dx (additivity over intervals); ∫ₐᵃ f dx = 0; ∫ₐᵇ f dx = -∫ᵇₐ f dx (reversing limits).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Probability Distributions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Probability Distributions?

options.text: Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals introduce integration as the reverse of differentiation.
The power rule: d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹ for any real n.
Differential Equation Basics introduces equations involving derivatives.
The expected value (mean) is μ = ∫₋∞^∞ x·f(x)dx, and the variance is σ² = ∫₋∞^∞ (x-μ)²f(x)dx.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Probability Distributions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Probability Distributions?

options.text: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus connects differentiation and integration: d/dx[∫ₐˣ f(t)dt] = f(x).
Integration transforms discrete counting into continuous probability.
The sum, difference, product, quotient, and power laws allow limits to be computed piece by piece.
Limits and Continuity are the foundation of calculus.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Probability Distributions as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Probability Distributions?

options.text: Control systems engineering: inverse tangent functions model phase shifts in frequency response, with derivatives indicating sensitivity to frequency changes
Total rainfall accumulation: if r(t) is the rainfall rate at time t, then total accumulated rainfall by time x is R(x) = ∫₀ˣ r(t)dt
Scientific measurement: using increasingly precise instruments to estimate a true value—smaller measurement intervals approximate the 'limit' of the quantity
Quality control: using normal distribution integrals to determine the probability that a manufactured part falls within specifications (Six Sigma methodology)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Probability Distributions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Probability Distributions?

options.text: Financial mathematics: differentiating revenue functions R(x) to find marginal revenue and determine the optimal quantity to maximize profit
Insurance: actuaries use probability integrals to calculate expected claim amounts and set premium prices
AP exam problems frequently ask students to apply MVT to piecewise data: 'Given the table, must there be a value c where f'(c) = 3?'
Compound interest with variable rates: dA/dt for A = P·e^(∫r(t)dt) requires the chain rule combined with the FTC for variable-rate financial modeling

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Probability Distributions.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Probability Distributions applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Coulomb's law: electric force F = kq₁q₂/r² → ∞ as distance r → 0, creating a vertical asymptote that reflects the impossibility of zero separation between charges
Medical research: sketching dose-response curves to identify therapeutic windows (effective dose ranges) for drug development
Understanding S-curves in product adoption: inflection point marks the transition from rapid early adoption growth to market saturation deceleration
Weather forecasting: probability distributions of temperature, rainfall, and wind speed are computed via integration for probabilistic weather predictions

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Probability Distributions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Probability Distributions?

options.text: Quality control: using normal distribution integrals to determine the probability that a manufactured part falls within specifications (Six Sigma methodology)
Newton's method in GPS: iteratively solving nonlinear equations to determine position from satellite distance measurements
Long-term drug concentration models: horizontal asymptotes represent the steady-state drug level that the body approaches with regular dosing
Dam design: engineers compute total hydrostatic force on dam faces to determine required structural strength and materials

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Probability Distributions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Probability Distributions in practice?

options.text: Scientific measurement: using increasingly precise instruments to estimate a true value—smaller measurement intervals approximate the 'limit' of the quantity
Navigation: calculating bearing angles using arctan(Δy/Δx) and differentiating to find rate of angle change for tracking systems
Insurance: actuaries use probability integrals to calculate expected claim amounts and set premium prices
Population dynamics: given a birth rate minus death rate function, calculate total population change over a decade

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Probability Distributions.
